claim.content:
The low-energy physics of the two-dimensional electron system at filling fraction $\nu=\tfrac{1}{2}$ is described by a Fermi-liquid-like theory of pseudoparticles (composite objects obtained by binding an electron to two flux quanta), with a well-defined pseudoparticle Fermi momentum $k_F$ and an interaction-generated effective mass $m^*$ such that low-energy collective excitations can be analyzed using Fermi-liquid kinematics (for example, a quadratic dispersion with kinetic energy scale $\sim k_F^2/2m^*$); this statement defines $k_F$ as the wavevector at which the pseudoparticle occupation jumps in the idealized Fermi-liquid picture and defines $m^*$ phenomenologically as the effective quasiparticle mass entering the quadratic dispersion of pseudoparticle excitations.
      Halperin, Lee, and Read, Phys. Rev. B 47, 7312 (1993)
